skillName: Understand angle notation and label angles correctly
misconceptionSummary: Students struggle to identify which angle is meant when several angles share a vertex.

visuals.ruleText: Three letters are needed because multiple angles can share the same vertex
visuals.subText: The outer letters specify which two arms — removing ambiguity

steps.narration: When three or more lines meet at a point, there are multiple angles at that vertex. Simply saying 'the angle at B' is ambiguous. The three-letter notation removes that ambiguity by naming the specific two arms that form the angle.

lines.text: At vertex O, rays go to A, B, and C
∠AOB = angle between arms OA and OB
∠BOC = different angle, between arms OB and OC

steps.narration: At vertex O, rays go out to A, B, and C. Angle AOB is the opening between arm OA and arm OB. Angle BOC is a different opening — between OB and OC. The outer letters tell you which pair of arms to look at.

lines.text: ∠AOC spans from arm OA all the way to arm OC
∠AOB + ∠BOC = ∠AOC (if B is between A and C)
Three-letter notation makes these distinctions clear  ✓

steps.narration: Angle AOC spans from OA to OC — a larger angle that includes angle AOB and angle BOC combined. The three-letter notation acts as a precise address, specifying exactly which angle out of several at the same vertex.

visuals.label: Outer letters name the arms — they specify which of several angles at a vertex

steps.narration: Three angles at the same vertex O — but three different values. The outer letters select which pair of arms to measure between. That is why three letters are always used.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: assuming 'the angle at O' is unambiguous when multiple rays meet there. Three letters are needed — the outer letters specify which two arms. ∠AOB and ∠AOC are different angles at the same vertex O.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: When several rays meet at a vertex, there are multiple angles there. The three-letter notation removes ambiguity — the outer letters name the two arms, pinpointing exactly which angle is meant.
